docs.text: WSJ Deep Digest — 2026-07-08 Story selection from WSJ section feeds; every summary is researched and written from non-WSJ sources — no paywalled WSJ text is used. 7 stories per section. 💻 Tech 1. Apple to Spend $30 Billion on U.S.-Made Chips From Broadcom — Apple announced on July 8 a multiyear agreement worth more than $30 billion with Broadcom to design and produce custom wireless-connectivity chips in the United States. It is Apple's largest domestic manufacturing commitment to date and is expected to yield more than 15 billion U.S.-made chips over its life. The deal includes a roughly $1.5 billion expansion of Broadcom's Fort Collins, Colorado facility and runs via long-term contracts through 2031. It forms the biggest single piece of Apple's previously announced $600 billion, four-year U.S. investment plan. Broadcom shares jumped about 5% on the news. Sources: CNBC, TechCrunch, CNN. 2. China Says It Has Found Security Vulnerabilities in Anthropic's Claude Code — China's Ministry of Industry and Information Technology warned on July 8 that its cybersecurity platform flagged a "backdoor" vulnerability in Anthropic's Claude Code. It said versions released between April and June (roughly 2.1.91 through 2.1.196) contain a built-in monitoring mechanism that can transmit users' location and identity data to remote servers without consent. An Anthropic engineer characterized the feature as a March experiment meant to prevent account abuse by unauthorized resellers and to detect users tied to Chinese AI labs. In response, Alibaba reportedly ordered employees to stop using Anthropic tools for work starting July 10. The dispute lands amid broader U.S.–China friction over AI tooling and data security. Sources: CNBC, CBS News, SCMP. 3. Nokia's New Act: Supplying the AI Data Center Boom — Nokia has repositioned itself as a key networking supplier for the AI data-center buildout, riding surging demand from AI and cloud customers to its highest stock price in more than 16 years. First-quarter orders from AI and cloud customers reached roughly €1 billion, with revenue from that group up about 49% year over year, led by its optical-networks business serving hyperscalers. On the strength of those results the company raised its full-year outlook for network infrastructure and elevated that unit to a growth segment. It also opened an AI Networking Innovation Lab at its Sunnyvale, California site to co-develop hardware for AI workloads. Sources: BigGo Finance, Data Center Dynamics. 4. AI Giants Are Handing Out Tons of Free Computing Power to Grab Startup Share — OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google are flooding early-stage startups with free compute and token credits to lock them into their ecosystems, competing on customer acquisition rather than price. OpenAI has offered packages such as $2 million in credits for equity plus a $500,000 no-equity tier; Anthropic countered with up to $500,000 (no equity, up sharply from a prior $30,000 offer); and Google Cloud offers up to $500,000 in credits plus early Gemini access. Y Combinator companies are especially targeted, and one founder — Dialogus' Hans Ibarra — said competing offers totaled more than $3 million in cloud and token credits. The strategy relies on lock-in: once a startup builds on one provider, migrating becomes costly and technically hard. It marks an escalation in the platform war for the next generation of AI-native companies. Sources: The Decoder, Newsquawk. 5. Microsoft Is Cutting More Than 3,000 Jobs in Xbox Division — Microsoft announced around July 6 that it is cutting roughly 4,800 jobs in total (about 2.1% of its workforce), with the Xbox division bearing the largest share. Xbox will lose about 3,200 positions phased in through July 2027, with roughly 1,600 taking effect immediately. The company also plans to spin off four studios — Compulsion Games, Double Fine, Ninja Theory, and Undead Labs. Xbox CEO Asha Sharma told staff the unit ran at margins three to 10 times lower than comparable platforms and lost 64 cents on every dollar invested in studios. The next-generation console (codenamed Helix, slated for late 2027/2028) remains on schedule. Sources: CNBC, CNN, TechSpot. 6. Big Tech Has Suddenly Flipped on the AI Jobs Wipeout Scenario — After a year of warnings that AI would wipe out large numbers of jobs, tech leaders have shifted to a more optimistic tone emphasizing that humans will stay central to AI-driven work. OpenAI's Sam Altman conceded the industry was "pretty wrong on the social and economic implications," while Anthropic's Dario Amodei — who warned in May 2025 that AI could eliminate half of entry-level jobs — has since highlighted more positive scenarios. An EY-Parthenon survey found the share of CEOs expecting AI to cause significant headcount reductions fell from about 46% in January 2025 to just 20% by May 2026. MIT economist David Autor attributed the shift either to the labor market not moving as fast as expected, or to leaders realizing it is bad business to say your product will destroy the economy. The reversal doesn't necessarily mean the jobs risk is gone — just that the messaging has changed. Sources: PYMNTS, Slashdot. 7. Forget Wall Street. Elite Students Are Spending Their Summers on Startup Dreams. — Elite university students are increasingly skipping finance, consulting, and Big Tech internships to launch AI startups, aided by new university-affiliated programs and a shakier entry-level job market. Examples include a Princeton student who turned down a Big Tech offer and a rocket-engineering role to build an AI startup in San Francisco, and Yale sophomore Leïa Ryan, who co-founded Cortex, a knowledge-systems startup for biolabs, instead of taking a biotech job. Supporting hubs include the alumni- and VC-backed Yale Hacker House (15 founders sharing a Nob Hill apartment) and TekTrek, a first-year incubator drawing mainly from MIT, Harvard, and Princeton at a temporary Presidio campus. A big driver is how much cheaper building software has become thanks to LLMs and open-source tools. The trend reflects a broader shift in where ambitious young talent sees the best risk-adjusted upside. Sources: Entrepreneur, Albert Pham. 📈 Markets & Finance 1. U.S. Stocks Decline After Trump Says Iran Ceasefire Is Over — President Trump declared the U.S.–Iran ceasefire "over," reigniting fears of regional escalation and threats to oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz. The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell about 623 points (1.2%) and the S&P 500 slipped 0.4%, while Germany's DAX dropped 2.2%. Oil surged roughly 6–7%, with Brent crude climbing to about $78.80 a barrel and WTI near $75, after Central Command said U.S. forces had struck more than 80 sites in Iran. The Strait of Hormuz carries roughly a fifth of the world's seaborne oil, so any disruption there feeds directly into inflation fears. Safe-haven assets firmed as investors braced for further military action. Sources: NBC News, CBS News. 2. SpaceX Shares Stumble in Nasdaq-100 Debut — SpaceX shares fell about 6.8% on their first day in the Nasdaq-100 (Tuesday, July 7), closing near $149.47 and sliding further to around $148 the next day — below the $150 level where the stock began trading on June 12. The drop disappointed investors who bet that index inclusion would draw guaranteed demand from funds tracking the benchmark, which manage roughly $800 billion in assets. Analysts noted much of that buying may already have been priced in after the stock ran up nearly 6% the prior week. A weak broader tape — the Nasdaq Composite fell 1.2% on semiconductor weakness — added pressure. Still, Wall Street stayed bullish, with Deutsche Bank setting a $255 target citing reusable rockets and satellite-internet growth. Sources: CNBC, Al Jazeera. 3. Samsung Fails to Impress, Amazon Issues AI Bonds and Oil Jumps — Samsung Electronics posted a roughly 19-fold (about 1,800%) surge in Q2 operating profit to around $58 billion, but its shares still fell about 7–8% as the results missed lofty AI expectations and sparked profit-taking after a ~150% run this year. The disappointment dragged chip peers lower, with Micron down 4.7% and AMD, Broadcom, and the SMH semiconductor ETF (-3%) all falling. Oil jumped after Iran attacked a Qatari LNG tanker near the Strait of Hormuz, sending Brent up about 3% to $74.16 and WTI up nearly 3% to $70.44. The session's other theme was corporate AI financing, as large tech issuers tapped debt markets to fund infrastructure buildouts. The Nasdaq Composite fell 1.16% to 25,818.69 and the S&P 500 slid 0.45% to 7,503.85. Sources: Marketplace, CNBC. 4. World's Hottest Market Risks Becoming a Squid Game (analysis) — The piece refers to South Korea's stock market, the year's best performer, with the KOSPI up roughly 165% over the past year. The concern is that extreme retail speculation could leave small investors holding steep losses if the rally reverses. Retail traders poured about ₩1.61 trillion ($1.1 billion) into leveraged ETFs tied to Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix, and the market logged a record 29,357 volatility-interruption triggers in the first half. Meanwhile foreign investors sold heavily, pulling out on a net basis with roughly $30 billion exiting in June alone. The "Squid Game" framing warns that a euphoric, speculation-driven run rarely ends well for latecomers. Sources: Seoul Economic Daily, BigGo Finance. 5. U.S. Stocks Retreat on Worries About AI Trade, Hormuz Fears — All three major U.S. indices closed lower on July 7, led by tech on doubts about the durability of the AI rally. The Nasdaq Composite fell 1.16% to 25,818.69, the S&P 500 dropped 0.45% to 7,503.85, and the Dow lost 0.25% to 52,925.15. The selling began in Asia, where South Korea's Kospi tumbled nearly 5% after Samsung fell almost 7%, and was compounded by a report that China's DeepSeek is developing its own AI chip. Oil rose after Iran struck a Qatari LNG tanker near the Strait of Hormuz — a waterway handling about 20% of the world's oil traffic — after the White House revoked Iran's oil-sales license. Taken together, the session captured the market's two dominant anxieties: stretched AI valuations and Middle East supply risk. Sources: Detroit News, MarketScreener. 6. GM-Backed Momenta Makes Muted Hong Kong Debut After $751 Million IPO — Momenta Global, the Chinese autonomous-driving firm backed by General Motors and Tencent, raised about $751 million (HK$5.89 billion) at HK$295.60 per share, valuing it near $9 billion. Shares rose as much as 3% at Wednesday's open but faded to close about 2.1% below the IPO price, a reality check amid the broader tech selloff. The company drew 14 cornerstone investors — including Mercedes-Benz, BlackRock, and Singapore's GIC — committing roughly $376 million. Founded in 2016 by former Microsoft researcher Cao Xudong, Momenta supplies automakers with software for steering, braking, lane changes, and parking. It posted a 2025 net loss of 3.46 billion yuan on revenue up 82.1% to 2.41 billion yuan. Sources: CNBC, Benzinga. 7. Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk (sector roundup) — Semiconductors drove the sector, with a sharp selloff on July 7–8 that erased more than a trillion dollars in market value as investors questioned the sustainability of record AI capital spending. Samsung's earnings triggered the rout: Intel and Applied Materials each dropped about 10%, AMD fell 8%, and the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) slid roughly 6%. Adding pressure was a report that China's DeepSeek is building its own AI chip, which would threaten incumbents' pricing power. Several analysts characterized the drawdown as a "mid-cycle reset" rather than the end of the AI trade. They maintained high price targets on bellwethers like Nvidia and Micron. Sources: Yahoo Finance/247 Wall St., Forbes. 💰 Personal Finance 1. A Couple Living on One Income Wants to Buy a Home. Can They Afford It? (advice — underlying topic) — A common affordability benchmark is the 28/36 rule: keep housing costs (principal, interest, taxes, insurance, and HOA) at or below 28% of gross monthly income and total debt at or below 36%, though many lenders permit debt-to-income ratios up to about 43–45% on conventional loans. Single-income households are typically advised to stress-test their budget against just one wage, since one salary must cover the mortgage plus emergencies. As a rough 2026 example, a $500,000 home with 20% down at a ~6.5% 30-year fixed rate runs about $2,528/month in principal and interest, requiring roughly $125,000 in annual income to stay within the 28% limit. Beyond the payment, buyers should budget for property taxes, insurance, maintenance, and a healthy emergency fund. On one income, keeping a larger cash cushion matters more because there's no second paycheck to absorb a job loss. Sources: Bankrate, Chase. 2. What It's Like to Retire and Start a Business in America (advice — underlying topic) — "Encore entrepreneurship" is a real and growing trend: self-employment among Americans 65 and older rose from 13.0% in 2013 to 16.3% in 2023, according to the SBA Office of Advocacy. Adults aged 55–64 now account for more than a quarter of U.S. startup activity. Research cited by AARP finds a 50-year-old founder is roughly 2.8 times more likely to launch a successful startup than a 25-year-old, thanks to deeper experience, networks, and capital. Older founders are typically motivated by purpose, flexibility, legacy, and supplemental income to extend financial security in retirement. Financial planners caution retirees to cap how much retirement savings they put at risk and to keep the venture from jeopardizing essential income. Sources: AARP, BusinessInitiative. 3. 3 Questions to Ask Yourself About Your First 401(k) (advice — underlying topic) — First-time enrollees should weigh three things. First, contribute at least enough to capture the full employer match — a common formula is 100% on the first 3% plus 50% on the next 2%, so contributing 5% earns a 4% match (effectively free money). Second, choose between Roth (contributions taxed now, withdrawals tax-free) and traditional (pre-tax now, taxed in retirement) based on whether you expect higher taxes later. Third, decide how much to contribute against the 2026 limits: $24,500 in employee deferrals, plus an $8,000 catch-up at 50+ (or $11,250 for ages 60–63), with a combined employee-plus-employer cap of $72,000. Note a 2026 rule change: catch-up contributions for those earning over $145,000 must generally be Roth. Sources: Fidelity — limits, Fidelity — match. 4. Strategy Logs $8.3 Billion Loss as It Sells Off Bitcoin — Strategy (formerly MicroStrategy) reported an $8.32 billion loss on its digital-asset holdings for Q2 2026 amid Bitcoin's sharp decline from its October 2025 peak. In a major reversal from its long-standing buy-and-hold approach, the company sold 3,588 BTC for about $216 million between June 29 and July 5 at average prices near $59,000–$61,000 — below its roughly $75,476 average cost basis. It used the proceeds to cover preferred-stock distributions and rebuild cash reserves. Strategy now holds about 843,775 Bitcoin, still the largest corporate stash. Its shares slipped on the news as investors weighed whether the company's leveraged bitcoin bet is starting to strain. Sources: Investing.com, CoinDesk. 5. Trump's Brokerage Accounts Made Big Trades Around 'Liberation Day' Tariffs — A financial disclosure showed investment accounts owned by President Trump made 327 previously undisclosed stock purchases worth as much as $12.8 million on April 8, 2025 — one day before he announced a pause on his April 2 "Liberation Day" tariffs. The buying spree concentrated in mega-cap tech names like Apple, Alphabet, Amazon, Microsoft, and Nvidia, which rebounded sharply after the tariff reversal. That reversal helped send the S&P 500 up roughly 9.5% in one of its best days ever. Trump had posted it was a "GREAT TIME TO BUY!!" the day before the pause. The filing noted late-filing fees for transactions that had not been reported on time, adding to ethics scrutiny of the president's trading. Sources: NBC News, CNBC. 6. Stock Funds Rallied 17.1% in a Quarter That Made Investors' Heads Spin — The average diversified U.S. stock fund surged in a whipsaw quarter driven by tariff turmoil. After U.S. stocks fell nearly 12% between April 2 and April 8, hopes for softer trade deals sparked a rally that lifted the market about 25% from April 9 through quarter-end. The broad U.S. market rose more than 11% for the quarter — its best showing since Q3 2023. High-growth and cyclical categories led, with large-growth funds like Vanguard Growth (18.4%) and American Funds Growth Fund of America (18.1%) topping the largest funds. The episode is a reminder that trying to time such swings often backfires, since the sharpest gains clustered in just a few sessions. Sources: Morningstar, Morningstar review. 7. Small Stocks Are Having Their Biggest Run in Decades — The Russell 2000 small-cap index has surged roughly 22% in the first half of 2026, its best first half since 1991, as the AI-infrastructure boom broadened beyond mega-cap tech to smaller suppliers. Semiconductor and chip-equipment firms have been the standout winners, accounting for 16 of the index's 50 top performers — names like Aehr Test Systems, Ichor Holdings, and MaxLinear have each rallied more than 400%. The rally has been fueled by Federal Reserve rate cuts, a narrowing earnings gap between small and large caps, and strong technical momentum. From its April 8, 2025 low, the Russell 2000 is up about 55.8% through mid-2026. Small caps had lagged for years, so the rotation marks a notable shift in market leadership. Sources: CNBC, LSEG. --- Generated by the WSJ Deep Digest tool: WSJ RSS for story selection → web research over non-WSJ sources for depth. No paywalled WSJ text used.

WSJ Deep Digest — 2026-07-09 Today's stories, researched and summarized from non-WSJ sources: chip megadeals and an AI-spending arms race, oil and bonds whipsawing on a broken Iran ceasefire, and a KOSPI boom turning treacherous. 💻 Tech 1. We Heard From More Than 1,000 Readers on State Farm's Controversial AI Makeover — After State Farm's AI overhaul, roughly 900-1,000 readers weighed in with reactions ranging from "terrible" and "infuriating" to "it sucks," describing chatbots and automated systems, though some accepted AI as inevitable if it kept prices low. The controversy stems from CEO Jon Farney telling the insurer's roughly 19,000 sales agents at a May Las Vegas convention that existing contracts would be replaced, with agents staying past 2027 needing new deals tied to revised sales targets and mandated daily AI use. Under the new terms, agents would lose deferred compensation and health coverage and face performance-based commissions, with some agents saying the changes could cut income by as much as 40%. Agent reaction was swift and negative, with one calling it "a real slap in the face," and many readers said they would switch insurers if their local agent closed. Sources: Crain's Chicago Business, PYMNTS, Quartz. 2. Apple to Spend $30 Billion on U.S.-Made Chips From Broadcom — On July 8, 2026, Apple CEO Tim Cook and Broadcom CEO Hock Tan announced a multiyear agreement exceeding $30 billion covering more than 15 billion U.S.-made chips through at least 2031, Apple's largest single commitment under its American Manufacturing Program. Broadcom will invest $1.5 billion to expand and modernize its Fort Collins, Colorado facility, where it will produce advanced radio-frequency components including FBAR filters and wireless connectivity technologies used across Apple's products. The deal is part of Apple's broader $600 billion, four-year U.S. investment commitment aimed at reducing reliance on overseas manufacturing. Broadcom supplies the wireless connectivity chips that let Apple devices connect to Wi-Fi, cellular networks, and Bluetooth. Sources: CNBC, MacRumors, CNN Business. 3. China Says It Has Found Security Vulnerabilities in Anthropic's Claude Code — China's National Vulnerability Database warned on July 8, 2026 that Anthropic's Claude Code contains a "backdoor" that transmits user location and identity data to remote servers without consent, affecting versions 2.1.91 through 2.1.196 released between April and June, and urged users to uninstall or upgrade immediately. Anthropic confirmed it had embedded code to track user locations in an effort to stop illicit "distillation" of its models, and responded that users in China being told to uninstall were not supposed to be using the product in the first place. Alibaba has already banned employees from using Claude Code, directing them to its own Qoder platform instead. The dispute centers on whether the tracking mechanism is a genuine security vulnerability or a legitimate anti-piracy measure, reflecting broader U.S.-China AI tensions. Sources: CNBC, South China Morning Post, Cybernews. 4. China's Zhipu AI Shares Surge on $4 Billion Fundraising — Chinese AI model maker Zhipu, which trades as Knowledge Atlas Technology, raised about $4 billion in a Hong Kong share placement on Thursday, July 9, 2026, after its stock soared nearly 1,500% since its January listing. The company offered 19.8 million shares priced at HK$1,588 to HK$1,698 each, a discount of as much as 13% to the prior close, in what became the second-biggest equity offering in Hong Kong this year, just six months after its IPO. Despite the discounted placement, Zhipu shares surged 14.7% to HK$2,094 after completing the sale. Proceeds are earmarked for R&D including hiring, computing capacity, technical services, business expansion, and strategic M&A. Sources: Bloomberg, Invezz, Investing.com. 5. Nokia's New Act: Supplying the AI Data Center Boom — Nokia raised growth targets for its AI business after beating quarterly expectations, sending its shares to a 16-year high, with the stock up 73% in 2026 on its pivot into AI and data-center markets. Net sales grew 4% early in the year while its AI and cloud segment jumped 49%, booking €1 billion (about $1.16 billion) in orders from AI and cloud customers in a single quarter driven by hyperscaler data-center buildouts that rely on fiber-optic networking. Strategic moves underpinning the shift include the $2.3 billion 2024 acquisition of optical-networking firm Infinera, a $1 billion NVIDIA investment to develop AI-RAN technology, and the April 2025 arrival of CEO Justin Hotard from Hewlett Packard Enterprise. With Western nations restricting Huawei and ZTE, Nokia has positioned itself as effectively the last remaining Western sovereign-level supplier. Sources: Capacity, Data Centre Magazine, Phemex News. 6. Will Someone Finally Blink in the AI Spending War? — Google, Amazon, Microsoft, and Meta collectively plan to spend around $725 billion on capital expenditures in 2026, up roughly 77% from 2025's record $410 billion, with the five largest hyperscalers projected to spend $700 billion to $900 billion overall. Roughly 75%, or about $450 billion, is tied directly to AI infrastructure—servers, GPUs, and datacenters—pushing capital intensity to historically unthinkable levels, with some firms dedicating 45-57% of revenue to infrastructure. Analysts note that pulling back now carries its own catastrophic competitive risks in an increasingly "Darwinian" race, meaning few executives are willing to blink first. Goldman Sachs projects a combined $5.3 trillion in capex across the four largest hyperscalers from fiscal 2025 through 2030. Sources: Yahoo Finance, Forbes, IEEE ComSoc. 7. AI Giants Are Handing Out Tons of Free Computing Power to Grab Startup Share — OpenAI, Anthropic, and cloud providers are racing to outbid one another with free compute and token credits to lock in the next generation of enterprise customers, with Y Combinator startups especially targeted at credits worth roughly $500,000 each. OpenAI's Sam Altman offered $2 million in API credits to a YC cohort in exchange for equity via uncapped SAFE agreements, prompting Anthropic to counter with $500,000 in Claude credits and no equity requirement—a roughly 16x jump from its prior standard allocation. One founder, Dialogus's Hans Ibarra, said competing offers in some cases exceeded $3 million in combined cloud and token credits, while Nvidia separately began offering startups GPU access in exchange for revenue share. The strategy is a long-term land grab, since once a startup embeds its product deep in one ecosystem, migrating away becomes a costly technical headache. Sources: The Decoder, Business Standard, Invezz. 📈 Markets & Finance 1. Tobacco Stocks Are Coming in From the Cold (analysis — underlying topic) — Long treated as pariah investments, tobacco names have rallied as they pivot to "smoke-free" products, led by Philip Morris International, whose smoke-free lines reached 41.5% of net revenues in 2025 and grew 6.5% year-over-year. Sales of its Zyn nicotine pouches jumped 37% to 879.6 million cans, and PMI paid $2.7 billion to acquire U.S. rights to its IQOS heated-tobacco line from Altria, launching in Austin in March 2025. PMI raised its quarterly dividend 8.9% (to an annualized $5.88 per share) in September, its 18th consecutive year of increases, while Altria has lifted its payout 60 times in 56 years. The reduced-risk product transition, defensive cash flows and fat dividend yields have drawn income and value investors back to a sector many funds had shunned. Sources: Motley Fool, SEC/PMI 8-K. 2. Wall Street's Peace Trade Collides With Renewed Iran Fighting — A "peace trade" that had lifted equities after a U.S.-Iran ceasefire unraveled in early July 2026 as renewed hostilities in the Middle East pushed oil sharply higher. After the initial truce, markets had rallied — the S&P 500 rose about 2.5%, the Nasdaq 2.8% and crude sold off hard — but the reversal sent Brent crude up more than 5% and battered risk assets. On the renewed-fighting session, the Dow fell about 576 points (roughly 1.1%) as investors unwound bets on de-escalation. Airline shares were hit hard on fears of higher fuel costs and disrupted travel, with American Airlines down nearly 4% and United off about 2.5%. European bourses also slid, with Germany's DAX and France's CAC 40 each losing roughly 2.2%. Sources: NBC News, Yahoo Finance. 3. U.S. Stocks Decline After Trump Says Iran Ceasefire Is Over — U.S. equities fell on Wednesday, July 8, 2026, after President Trump told a NATO summit in Turkey that the ceasefire with Iran was "over," reigniting fears of a wider Middle East war. The Dow Jones Industrial Average dropped 576.76 points, or 1.09%, to close at 52,348.39, and the S&P 500 slipped 0.28% to 7,482.71, though the Nasdaq Composite bucked the trend, edging up 0.2% to 25,870.65. Oil surged on the news, with Brent crude settling up 5.43% at $78.19 a barrel and West Texas Intermediate rising 4.37% to $73.52. Airline stocks led the decline on fuel-cost and demand worries, with American Airlines down nearly 4%. The U.S. subsequently signaled it would resume strikes on Iran, keeping markets on edge. Sources: CNBC, Motley Fool. 4. Samsung Fails to Impress, Amazon Issues AI Bonds and Oil Jumps | Markets P.M. for July 7 — Three currents drove markets on July 7, 2026. Samsung Electronics flagged a roughly 19-fold jump in second-quarter operating profit to about 89.4 trillion won ($58.4 billion) on AI-driven memory demand, but shares still fell as much as 10% before closing down 6.9% in Seoul, as a near-150% run-up had already priced in the blockbuster and softer-than-hoped DRAM pricing spooked investors. Amazon launched an eight-part bond offering to raise at least $25 billion for AI infrastructure and data centers, but had to add 18–21 basis points of extra yield on its longest notes as orders came in at just 2.5 times supply — its weakest hyperscaler reception since Meta's $30 billion sale in October 2025. Amazon expects capital spending to reach roughly $200 billion this year, up from $131 billion in 2025. Oil jumped on renewed Iran tensions, with the Dow sliding more than 570 points as Brent pushed toward $78. Sources: Yahoo Finance/Reuters, CNBC. 5. SpaceX Shares Stumble in Nasdaq-100 Debut — SpaceX joined the Nasdaq-100 on Tuesday, July 7, 2026 — less than a month after its June 12 market debut, among the fastest inclusions ever under revised Nasdaq rules — but the stock slid rather than rallied. Shares fell about 5.4% amid a broader pullback in high-momentum tech names, and closed at $148 on Wednesday, below its $150 first-trade price for a second straight day. The drop came despite bullish Wall Street initiations, including Morgan Stanley "overweight" with a $300 target, Bernstein "outperform" at $239, RBC "outperform" at $225 and UBS "buy" at $210. History cautioned against expecting an index-inclusion pop: over the prior two years, only 6 of 21 companies added to the Nasdaq-100 saw their stock rise in the first week. The roughly $2 trillion rocket-and-satellite firm's inclusion is still expected to trigger billions in passive buying. Sources: CNBC, Al Jazeera. 6. Private-Equity Firms Are Sitting on a Nine-Year Backlog — Buyout firms would need roughly nine years to clear their inventory of unsold portfolio companies at the current exit pace, according to a PwC analysis of PitchBook data. About 13,500 U.S. companies sat in PE portfolios as of June 30, 2026, with nearly 4,000 held six-plus years and about 1,500 held nine or more years; Bain & Company pegged the global buyout backlog at roughly 32,000 companies worth about $3.8 trillion. Average holding periods have stretched to around seven years at exit, up from five to six during 2010–2021. The overhang matters because stalled exits choke the distributions that pensions, endowments and family offices rely on to fund fresh commitments. Worries about AI's disruption of the software industry — a core PE hunting ground — are deepening the freeze. Sources: The Daily Upside/Yahoo, Financier Worldwide. 7. World's Hottest Market Risks Becoming a Squid Game — South Korea's KOSPI, 2026's best-performing major index after roughly doubling year-to-date, has turned treacherous, tumbling into bear-market territory in early July. The index fell more than 5% on Wednesday, July 8, leaving it about 20% below its June 19 record high, as the chip-fueled rally cracked. The gains — and the pain — are dangerously concentrated: SK Hynix and Samsung Electronics together account for more than half the index's value, so a wobble in AI memory pricing hits the whole market. Retail traders known as "ants" have been the market's backstop, buying dips and piling into leveraged ETFs, including exposure routed through Hong Kong platforms, leaving novice investors especially vulnerable in a downturn. The biggest external risk is that U.S. hyperscalers such as Microsoft, Apple and Amazon rein in AI spending faster than expected, undercutting Korean chip demand. Sources: CNBC, Al Jazeera. 💰 Personal Finance 1. 3 Questions to Ask Yourself About Your First 401(k) (advice — underlying topic) — When opening a first 401(k), advisers say the three questions that matter most are whether you're contributing enough to capture the full employer match, whether to choose Roth (after-tax) or traditional (pre-tax) contributions, and how your money is invested and vested. Not contributing enough to earn a match — commonly a 100% match up to 4%, or 50% up to 6% — effectively leaves free compensation on the table. The Roth-versus-traditional choice hinges on whether you expect a higher tax rate in retirement than today, since Roth withdrawals are tax-free while traditional contributions defer taxes now. Vesting schedules matter too: your own contributions are always 100% yours, but employer matches may take years to fully vest, so leaving early can forfeit that money. Sources: Indeed, Charles Schwab, Empower. 2. A Couple Living on One Income Wants to Buy a Home. Can They Afford It? (advice — underlying topic) — The standard benchmark for single-income home affordability in 2026 remains the 28/36 rule: keep total housing costs (principal, interest, taxes, insurance and HOA fees) at or below 28% of gross monthly income, and all debt payments under 36%. There is no fixed minimum income to qualify — lenders instead weigh debt-to-income ratio, employment stability, credit score and loan type, with most conventional loans requiring a credit score of at least 620 and down payments ranging from as little as 0% (VA) or 3.5% (FHA) to conventional minimums. Because one earner carries the entire mortgage, advisers stress building a robust emergency fund to cover repairs or a loss of income. The rules of thumb are only starting points: buyers should evaluate their full financial picture and whether a payment will still feel comfortable years down the road. Sources: Opendoor, Loan Pronto, Freedom Mortgage. 3. Strategy Logs $8.3 Billion Loss as It Sells Off Bitcoin — Strategy (formerly MicroStrategy) reported an $8.32 billion loss on digital assets for the quarter ended June 30, 2026, after selling 3,588 bitcoin for less than it paid. Tech Stock Jitters Just Went Off the Charts — A sharp repricing of AI and chip stocks rattled markets in early-to-mid July 2026 as investors questioned valuations built more on the technology's promise than on bottom-line profit growth. MiniMax Shares Drop on $2 Billion Fundraising Plan — Shares of Chinese AI startup MiniMax tumbled after it unveiled a roughly $2 billion capital raise combining equity and debt. The Mind Game That Investors Can't Stop Playing — The market-timing impulse — trying to sell before drops and buy back before rebounds — is one of the most persistent and costly behavioral traps in investing. It is rooted in loss aversion, the tendency identified by psychologists Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky in which the pain of a loss is roughly twice as powerful as the pleasure of an equivalent gain, pushing investors to panic-sell in downturns and chase rallies at the top. Research suggests emotional decision-making shaves 1–2 percentage points off returns annually, and studies of the "behavior gap" find the average investor earns only about 3–4% versus roughly 10% for the market itself. Because the market's best days often cluster right after its worst, investors who flee downturns tend to miss the recovery, locking in losses that compound over time — which is why disciplined buy-and-hold strategies usually win. Increasingly, wearables and smart-home sensors round out the plan, but the recurring advice is to act proactively rather than waiting until after a fall. Supply has loosened to roughly 4.5 months — still tilted toward sellers but far from the ultra-tight 2–3 month market of 2021–2022 — and forecasters expect single-family and condo listings to rise 5–10% in 2026 as homeowners respond to steadier rates. The result is a more balanced market in which well-priced homes still move but buyers have regained negotiating room, inspection contingencies, and time to decide. Sources: Yahoo Finance, J.P. Morgan, National Association of Realtors. 4. A key culprit is the tiny public float: SpaceX raised about $86 billion in its offering but floated less than 5% of the company, limiting how much index-tracking funds could buy. Fundamentals also weigh on sentiment — SpaceX posted a $4.9 billion net loss in 2025 and a $4.3 billion loss in the first quarter of 2026, much of it tied to xAI, the AI startup it absorbed earlier in the year. Sources: CNBC, Al Jazeera, The Motley Fool. 5. Parents of Some Babies Face Delays Opening Trump Accounts — The government's new "Trump Accounts" — which seed a $1,000 federal deposit for U.S.-citizen children born between January 1, 2025 and December 31, 2028 — launched on July 4, 2026, but some parents hit early snags because the required paperwork wasn't immediately available. IRS Form 4547 and the online portal at TrumpAccounts.gov did not go fully live until around July 5, delaying families eager to open accounts and begin adding contributions on top of the government seed.
